title: AR-RAG: Autoregressive Retrieval Augmentation for Image Generation
methodology: The authors propose patch-level k-nearest-neighbor retrieval performed autoregressively at each step of image generation, implemented either via a training-free distribution-merging strategy (DAiD) that blends model and retrieval token distributions, or via a parameter-efficient fine-tuning approach (FAiD) that smooths retrieved patch features through multi-scale convolutions and adaptively blends them into transformer hidden states.
key_findings: AR-RAG (both DAiD and FAiD) outperforms state-of-the-art non-retrieval and image-level retrieval baselines on GenEval, DPG-Bench, and Midjourney-30K benchmarks, with Janus-Pro+FAiD achieving 6.67 FID on Midjourney-30K and 0.78 overall on GenEval.

Conventional image-level retrieval augmentation methods (RDM, ImageRAG) often perform worse than their non-retrieval counterparts because they over-copy irrelevant visual elements and bias compositional layout, sometimes introducing hallucinated visual content (e.g., a basketball player from a reference image).

Patch-level autoregressive retrieval dynamically adapts to the evolving generation context, allowing selective incorporation of fine-grained visual references while avoiding over-copying, stylistic bias, and hallucination of irrelevant elements seen in static retrieval methods.
DAiD adds only ~0.22% inference overhead (training-free) while FAiD adds ~36% overhead but yields the largest quality gains; both reduce local distortions in complex objects and multi-object scenes.
Improvements are largest on prompts requiring multi-object composition and spatial relations (e.g., GenEval 'Two Obj.' and 'Position' categories), where static retrievals tend to fail.
limitations: The work focuses exclusively on image generation rather than text/LLM hallucinations; findings about reducing hallucination apply to visual artifacts (irrelevant objects, distortions) not factual LLM hallucinations.
FAiD requires fine-tuning and adds ~36% inference latency, which may limit deployment.
Evaluation is restricted to autoregressive image generation backbones (Janus-Pro, Show-o); generalization to diffusion models is not directly demonstrated.
Performance depends on the quality and coverage of the patch-level retrieval database (CC12M, JourneyDB, DataComp), and the paper does not deeply analyze failure modes when no relevant patches exist.
relevance_reasoning: The paper applies retrieval augmentation to an autoregressive generative model and discusses 'hallucination' of irrelevant visual elements, but it targets image generation rather than factual hallucinations in text-based LLMs. Its mechanisms (patch-level k-NN retrieval, distribution/feature blending) are conceptually analogous to LLM RAG but the findings do not directly address LLM hallucinations.
